content.full_text:
January 20,
1993
Linda Moulton-Patterson
California Coastal Commission
501 20th Street
Huntington Beach,
CA
92648
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-637
Dear Ms.
Moulton—Patterson:
You have requested advice regarding your duties as a member
of the California Coastal Commission under the conflict—of—
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
Under the Act,
may you participate in the following Coastal
Commission decisions:
1.
The city of Port Hueneme is the applicant for
discretionary coastal permits including a general plan
amendment,
zone changes and other related actions
pertaining to a recreational vehicle resort.
A partner
in the same law firm as your husband,
serves as the
contract city attorney.
2.
A land developer is the applicant for a discretionary
coastal permit.
The coastal project which is the
subject of the application is located in the City of
Dana Point.
Your husband is the contract city attorney.
CONCLUSIONS
1 and 2.
You may participate in the decisions if there will
not be a material financial effect on the law firm,
which is a
source of income to you.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-92-637
Page
2
FACTS
You serve on the city council for the City of Huntington
Beach and are also an appointed member of the California Coastal
Commission.
Your husband, Jerry M. Patterson,
is an equity
partner in the law firm of Burke,
Williams
& Sorenson
(“law
firm”).
He has an ownership interest of less than five percent in
the law firm.
The law firm serves as contract city attorney for
several coastal cities.
Mr. Patterson is the city attorney for
the City of Dana Point.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a governmental deci
sion within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef
fect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on
the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dol
lars
($250)
or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within
12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.


Our File No.
A-92-637
Page
3
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
Section 87103
Accordingly,
you may not make, participate in making,
or
attempt to use your official position to influence a governmental
decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on any of your economic interests,
including the
law firm,
which is a source of income to you.
From the facts which you have provided, it does not appear
that either decision will have a financial effect on the law firm.
However, if these,
or any other decisions, will have a financial
effect on the law firm,
you must determine under Regulation
18702.2
(copy enclosed)
if the effect is material.
Also,
if your
husband’s ownership interest in the firm increases to ten percent
or more, additional analysis would be necessary.
(Section 82030(a).)
I trust this answers your questions.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Assistant Genral Counsel
By:
:e
e1,
Legal Division
SH: SJ:dl
enc.

may you participate in the following Coastal
Commission decisions:
1.
The city of Port Hueneme is the applicant for
discretionary coastal permits including a general plan
amendment,
zone changes and other related actions
pertaining to a recreational vehicle resort.
A partner
in the same law firm as your husband,
serves as the

coastal permit.
The coastal project which is the
subject of the application is located in the City of
Dana Point.
Your husband is the contract city attorney.

CONCLUSION: 1 and 2.

which is a

6 of the California Code of Regulations.
